ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product class; no specific entry for Swiss-sauce braised preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Swiss-sauce braised chicken wings. A00LC (Poultry meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 chicken mid-wings, approximately 120 g total, as typically served at a Hong Kong-style bing sutt or kopitiam in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken wing, cooked, braised — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05072) and HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for soy-braised chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken wing (skin on, braised). Sodium and sugar adjusted upward to reflect Swiss sauce preparation (soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar syrup). HPB Singapore data for soy-braised chicken dishes used as cross-reference for sodium and sugar estimates. Final per-100g values represent a composite estimate for Swiss-sauce braised chicken mid-wings.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and soy-sauce braised dishes — analogous estimate based on soy-braised pork and chicken entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Swiss-sauce braised chicken wings. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous soy-braised meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with modest sugar content from the sauce; the low GI reflects the negligible starch content and moderate sugar from soy-sauce glaze. GL per serving calculated as (5.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2 serving factor × 35) / 100 ≈ 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and dark soy sauce in Swiss sauce preparation
hypertension.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 744 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate added sugar from Swiss sauce glaze with low overall glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low GI (~35) due to its high protein and fat content, but the Swiss sauce contributes approximately 5.4 g sugar per 100 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and be aware of the cumulative sugar load from the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from braised chicken skin and soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 120 g serving provides approximately 4.3 g saturated fat and 744 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider skinless preparation to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Swiss Sauce Chicken Mid-Wings (瑞士汁鸡中翼) is a classic Hong Kong-style braised chicken dish widely served at bing sutts (Hong Kong-style cafes) and kopitiams across Singapore. Despite the name, 'Swiss sauce' has no connection to Switzerland; the name is believed to derive from a Cantonese mispronunciation of 'sweet' (甜, tím), referring to the characteristic sweet-savoury soy glaze. The dish consists of chicken mid-wings slow-braised in a sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, water, garlic, and aromatics such as star anise, producing a glossy, mahogany-coloured coating. The texture is tender and succulent with a slightly sticky, caramelised exterior. The flavour profile is deeply savoury with a pronounced sweetness balanced by umami from the soy base. Nutritionally, each 120 g serving (2 mid-wings) provides approximately 262 kcal, 23.4 g protein, 15.8 g fat, and 7.0 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~744 mg per serving) due to the soy-sauce-heavy braising liquid, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~35) owing to the dominant protein and fat matrix, though the added sugar in the sauce warrants attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is a popular hawker and casual dining item in Singapore, commonly ordered as a side dish or as part of a set meal at Cantonese-style establishments.
common_user_queries: What is Swiss sauce chicken wings?
Why is it called Swiss sauce if it is not from Switzerland?
How many calories in Swiss sauce chicken wings?
Is Swiss sauce chicken wings healthy?
How much sodium in Swiss sauce chicken wings?
Can diabetics eat Swiss sauce chicken wings?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Swiss-sauce braised chicken wings was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked braised chicken wing data with sodium and sugar adjusted upward to reflect the Swiss sauce preparation, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore soy-braised chicken data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation required.
notes: 1. The input food name '瑞士汁鸡中亦（2只）' contains a likely OCR or typographic error: '亦' should be '翼' (wing). The canonical Chinese name has been corrected to '瑞士汁鸡中翼（2只）' in simplified and '瑞士汁雞中翼（2隻）' in traditional. The original erroneous form is preserved in aliases for retrieval purposes. 2. Serving size set at 120 g (2 mid-wings with sauce) based on typical kopitiam portion weight. 3. Sodium_mg is elevated (620 mg/100g) reflecting soy-sauce-heavy Swiss sauce; this is a key clinical concern. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. 7. Halal status is marked false as the dish is typically served at non-halal certified bing sutts; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. GI estimated at 35 (low) from analogous soy-braised meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement found for this specific dish. 9. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as chicken wing braised in soy sauce contains negligible vitamin C, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked poultry.

Added traditional Chinese form '瑞士汁雞中翼（2隻）' to local_names.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Singapore Chinese cuisine.
Derived per_100g nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy braised chicken wing data with sodium and sugar adaptation for Swiss sauce.
Set GI to 35 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-not-reported justification in notes.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy cooked poultry data.
Ensured aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent by adding 'Ruì shì zhī jī zhōng yì (2 zhī)' to the flat aliases array, resolving the q8 failure.
